---
title: "Lecture 33: Regression Models for Trend and Seasonality"
subtitle: 161.251 Regression Modelling
author: "Presented by Nick Knowlton <N.Knowlton@massey.ac.nz>"  
date: "Week 11 of Semester 2, `r lubridate::year(lubridate::now())`"
output:
  html_document:
    code_download: true
    theme: sandstone
    highlight_style: pygments
  html_notebook:
    code_download: true
    theme: sandstone
    highlight_style: pygments
  ioslides_presentation:
    widescreen: true
    smaller: true
  word_document: default
  slidy_presentation: 
    theme: sandstone
    highlight_style: pygments
  pdf_document: default
---




<!--- Data is on
https://r-resources.massey.ac.nz/data/161251/
--->

```{r setup, purl=FALSE, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(dev=c("png", "pdf"))
opts_chunk$set(fig.height=6, fig.width=7, fig.path="Figures/", fig.alt="unlabelled")
opts_chunk$set(comment="", fig.align="center", tidy=TRUE)
options(knitr.kable.NA = '')
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(tsibble)
library(fable)
library(feasts)
library(lubridate)
```


<!--- Do not edit anything above this line. --->
```{r trend-seasonality-setup, include=FALSE}
library(lubridate)
```

## Regression Models for Trend and Seasonality

When observations are recorded over time, the mean structure may change systematically. In this lecture we continue the Seoul Bike example, but now move to a daily aggregate so that the time pattern is easier to see.

By the end of this lecture, students should be able to:

1. Use trend terms to model long-term movement.
2. Use indicator variables to represent seasonal effects.
3. Build sinusoidal and Fourier terms for periodic behaviour.
4. Interpret the final model as a regression description of the mean over time.

`r xfun::embed_file("../Data/seoul_bike_daily.csv")`

```{r read seoul daily, message=FALSE}
seoul_daily = read_csv("../Data/seoul_bike_daily.csv")
seoul_daily
```

### A Regression View of Time Structure

We focus on the mean function:

- `day_index` captures long-term change,
- `season` captures a coarse repeating pattern,
- sine and cosine terms provide a compact seasonal representation,
- weather variables can still matter after we account for time.

We begin by assuming residual independence so that we can concentrate on building the mean structure.

### Trend Alone

```{r trend-only, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
ggplot(seoul_daily, aes(x = day_index, y = rented_bike_count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    x = "Day index",
    y = "Daily rented bike count",
    title = "Daily bike demand changes over time"
  )
```

```{r trend-model}
seoul_trend = lm(rented_bike_count ~ day_index, data = seoul_daily)
summary(seoul_trend)
```

A simple trend captures some of the structure, but clearly not all of it.

### Season Indicators

```{r season-model}
seoul_season = lm(rented_bike_count ~ day_index + season, data = seoul_daily)
summary(seoul_season)
```

This is the simplest regression-first way to say that the mean level differs by season.

### Fourier Terms for Seasonality

For a yearly cycle we can build sine and cosine terms:

$$S_1(t) = \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{365}\right), \qquad C_1(t) = \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{365}\right).$$

```{r seoul fourier setup}
seoul_daily = seoul_daily |>
  mutate(
    S1 = sin(2 * pi * day_index / 365),
    C1 = cos(2 * pi * day_index / 365),
    S2 = sin(4 * pi * day_index / 365),
    C2 = cos(4 * pi * day_index / 365)
  )
```

```{r fourier-model}
seoul_fourier = lm(
  rented_bike_count ~ day_index + S1 + C1 + S2 + C2,
  data = seoul_daily
)
summary(seoul_fourier)
```

These Fourier terms provide a more systematic alternative to using many separate seasonal indicators.

### Adding Weather Back Into the Mean

Even after accounting for time, weather still matters.

```{r seoul mean model}
seoul_mean = lm(
  rented_bike_count ~ day_index + S1 + C1 + S2 + C2 +
    mean_temperature + total_rainfall + total_solar_radiation,
  data = seoul_daily
)
summary(seoul_mean)
```

```{r mean model plot, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
seoul_daily |>
  mutate(fitted = fitted(seoul_mean)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = day_index, y = rented_bike_count)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(aes(y = fitted), colour = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Day index",
    y = "Daily rented bike count",
    title = "A regression model for trend, seasonality, and weather"
  )
```

## A Brief Diagnostic Look

```{r residual look}
plot(residuals(seoul_mean) ~ seoul_daily$day_index)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)
```

The fitted mean structure is much richer than a simple line, but it may still leave systematic dependence in the residuals. That is the question for the next lecture.
